items.title: AI wealth gap splits SF: ~10,000 people making $20M+, rest watching from outside
items.idea: Michael Parekh synthesizes a cluster of reporting: a VC viral X post, TechCrunch haves-and-have-nots, and The Decoder. Around 10,000 people at OpenAI, Anthropic, and Mag-7 AI divisions generating outsized short-term wealth ($20M+). The much larger group inside the same companies, same buildings, same zip codes watches from outside. Parallel to 1995-2000 Internet wave but numbers are larger and social media makes visibility constant and inescapable.
items.angle: The wage gap is emotionally distinct because it's not tech vs non-tech — it's people doing similar-looking work separated only by which company or team they landed on. That resentment changes behavior: resignation rates, risk appetite, migration patterns.
items.tweetA: The AI wealth gap isn't tech vs. non-tech. It's OpenAI vs. everyone else in the same building. ~10,000 people in SF are making $20M+. The next 100,000 tech workers are watching it happen on LinkedIn in real time. That resentment is real — and it changes behavior. People leave. People take more risk. That's the second-order story nobody is pricing.
items.tweetB: Every tech wave creates this: a tiny group winning enormous, a large group wondering why they bother. It happened in 1999. This time the numbers are bigger AND you can't escape it — your neighbors, your LinkedIn, your company Slack are all showing it. The Internet wave created founders. The AI wave is creating a two-class system inside the same building.

items.title: AI degrades college credential signal: if AI does the cognitive lifting, what does the diploma prove?
items.idea: NYT Opinion piece: first generation that grew up with ChatGPT is now graduating. A Stanford undergrad describes how AI makes it nearly impossible not to cut corners. Structural question: if AI does the cognitive lifting in every assignment, what does the diploma signal to employers anymore?
items.angle: Slow-motion disruption to a multi-trillion-dollar credentialing system. Alternative credentialing (portfolio work, demonstrated output, bootcamps) should accelerate as employers try to find a new signal.
items.tweetA: NYT asked the question nobody wants to answer: if AI does the cognitive work, what exactly does the diploma signal now? The first AI-native college class is graduating. I don't think employers have figured out the answer yet. But in 3 years they will. Watch what happens to the premium on elite degrees.
items.tweetB: The credential problem is brutal math. If everyone can generate A-quality work with AI, 'can produce A-quality work' stops being a signal. The signal shifts to judgment, taste, curation — things that can't be prompted. We don't know how to measure those yet. That's a $2T+ higher education sector disruption waiting to happen.

items.title: Sad wives of AI: Wired documents marriage strain from the 24/7 build cycle
items.idea: Wired piece on spouses of AI founders/engineers describing 24/7 AI build cycle. 'AI doesn't have a clear weekend. The model training doesn't pause.' The competitive frame against China makes the intensity feel existential, not just competitive. Wired covered similar stories in every prior tech wave, but this one runs at higher amplitude.
items.angle: Every sustained capital flow eventually creates social friction and demographic effects. 24/7 intensity at existential scale → delayed family formation → changed fertility patterns in AI-dense cities. The personal becomes macro over 5-10 years.
items.tweetA: Buried in this Wired piece on 'sad wives of AI': they're not venting. They're describing a structural reality. AI is being built by people for whom there is no weekend — the model doesn't stop training. That is the actual pace of the race. The people bearing it alongside the builders are telling us it's real and it's compounding.
items.tweetB: Silicon Valley has had the 'spouse of a founder' story in every wave. The AI version is different in one key way: the race against China makes the 24/7 pace feel existential, not just competitive. Existential pressure doesn't ease. The demographic and social consequences will take a decade to fully show up.

items.title: Amazon Alexa+ rolls out AI-generated podcasts on 100M+ Echo devices — massive distribution for AI audio
items.idea: Amazon rolling out Alexa+ AI-generated podcasts (NotebookLM conversational format) across hundreds of millions of Echo devices. This is the first massive hardware distribution moment for AI-generated audio content — Google/OpenAI/Anthropic don't have the kitchen-counter and bedside hardware footprint Amazon has.
items.angle: When AI-generated content gets native distribution through ambient computing devices already in people's homes, the economics of content creation change structurally. The question shifts from 'will people consume AI audio' to 'who can compete on volume when Amazon can generate infinite personalized content.'
items.tweetA: NotebookLM proved AI-generated podcasts work. Amazon now has the hardware to distribute them at scale — hundreds of millions of Echo devices in kitchens, bedrooms, everywhere. OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic don't have ambient hardware in your home. Amazon does. The AI content distribution question just got a new dimension.
items.tweetB: The AI audio moment nobody is tracking: Amazon Alexa+ rolling out NotebookLM-style podcasts at ~100M device scale. Content creation economics change when you can generate personalized audio for zero marginal cost and distribute through ambient hardware already in every room. Traditional podcast economics don't survive this at scale.

items.title: AI trade is a liquidity black hole: every spare dollar concentrated in one trade, no competing release valve
items.idea: Key structural difference between today and 2021: every marginal dollar of excess capital flows into the same AI trade. In 2021, excess liquidity dispersed across NFTs, watches, altcoins, meme stocks. Today there are no competing release valves at meaningful scale. 'The world's most perfectly designed number go up market is seeing zero competition from any other capital or excess liquidity release valve.' Only the Fed can stop what's happening.
items.angle: This framework explains simultaneously why the melt-up is more extreme than 2021 AND why it's more fragile. Concentration without a release valve = steeper ascent but no diversified cushion when the narrative breaks. Also explains crypto underperformance — every spare dollar is forced into $SMH.
items.tweetA: Here's the structural difference between this market and 2021: in 2021, excess capital went everywhere — NFTs, watches, altcoins, meme stocks. Today every spare dollar is forced into the same AI trade. No competing release valve. That makes the melt-up steeper, the positioning more concentrated, and the eventual unwind more violent. The black hole works both ways.
items.tweetB: 'Only the Fed can stop what's happening.' The AI trade is a liquidity black hole with no competition. Crypto is underperforming because every dollar that would have been a Solana pump is now in $SMH. Read this as simultaneously the bull thesis AND the crash thesis — the same flow that creates the melt-up creates the unwind when it reverses.

items.title: Global inflation surge: US PPI 6.0% (3-year high), CPI 3.8%, copper record $6.58/lb, April PPI 2nd largest monthly jump since 2010
items.idea: Synchronized global inflation across multiple data prints: US CPI 3.8% YoY (highest since May 2023), US PPI 6.0% YoY (highest since Jan 2023), April PPI +1.4% m/m (only March 2022 was larger since 2010), copper at record $6.58/lb, ground beef at all-time highs, China April PPI +2.8% YoY vs +1.6% estimate, EU raising inflation forecast citing Iran war 'stagflationary shock'. TIPS ETF saw $900M inflows in April — largest since Dec 2021.
items.angle: Not a US tariff story — it's simultaneous and coordinated global inflation at a time when no central bank has a credible tightening mandate. $900M TIPS inflow means institutional money is now explicitly hedging against inflation re-acceleration. The 'transitory again' narrative has quietly died.
items.tweetA: April US PPI +1.4% m/m — the 2nd largest monthly jump since 2010. Only March 2022 was bigger. CPI 3.8%, PPI 6.0%, copper at record $6.58/lb, ground beef at all-time highs. The market shrugged last week... then started giving back gains Friday and Monday. The digestion is beginning. The inflation swaps say this isn't done.
items.tweetB: The inflation problem is now officially global and synchronized. US CPI 3.8% (3-year high). China PPI +2.8% YoY (120bps above estimate). UK 30-year bonds at 1998 highs. EU raising inflation forecast citing Iran 'stagflationary shock.' This is not a US tariff story. This is a coordinated global inflation cycle. No central bank has a playbook for it.

items.title: Japan 40-year yield hits all-time high 4.41%; UK 30-year at 1998 levels — global bond vigilante moment
items.idea: Japan 40-year yield reached 4.41% — the highest in recorded history for a country running negative rates three years ago. UK 30-year yields at highest since 1998 ('Liz Truss moment becoming political reality'). 'No matter who is in power, no matter their political leaning, there does not appear to be a credible plan to restore the country's finances.' US 30-year at 5.15% post-2007 high.
items.angle: Japan is the critical watch. The BoJ's implicit curve management has been the backstop for the global 'rates eventually fall' narrative. Japan 40-year at 4.41% signals the long-end is beyond any soft intervention. Every asset priced on 'lower rates eventually' needs re-evaluation.
items.tweetA: Japan 40-year yield: 4.41% — the highest in recorded history. This is the country that was doing NEGATIVE rates three years ago. The BoJ's slow-motion loss of control of the long end is accelerating. If the country that invented yield curve control can't hold the long end, who can? And what does it mean for every asset priced on 'rates eventually fall'?
items.tweetB: UK 30-year at 1998 highs. Japan 40-year at all-time highs. US 30-year at post-2007 highs. These aren't unrelated. Fiscal math is catching up with governments that kept spending through the rate cycle. The bond market is the only mechanism that can actually say 'no.' And right now it's screaming it simultaneously in three major economies.

items.title: Consumer credit stress: auto delinquencies at 32-year high (5.6%), credit cards 2011 highs, real wages falling
items.idea: US consumer credit stress across all categories: auto loan delinquencies at record 5.6% (32-year high), credit card delinquencies at 13.1% (highest since 2011), student loan delinquencies at 10.3% (highest since 2020). Real wages fell in April despite Americans working extra hours to cover rising gas and food costs. These signals are flashing at the same time the cap-weighted S&P is near all-time highs.
items.angle: Equity market ignores consumer credit because AI stocks mask everything. But credit delinquencies are lagging-leading — they tell you what already happened to consumers while equities price future tech profits. Home Depot, Lowe's, Walmart prints this week are the reconciliation.
items.tweetA: Auto loan delinquencies: 32-year high. Credit card delinquencies: highest since 2011. Student loans: highest since 2020. All happening while the AI trade absorbs all attention and capital. The Home Depot and Walmart prints this week are going to show whether the consumer stress is contained or spreading. I know which way the delinquency data is pointing.
items.tweetB: Real wages fell in April even with Americans working extra hours. Auto delinquencies at 32-year highs, credit cards at 2011 highs — the 'resilient consumer' story is bifurcating hard. The top half is fine. The bottom half is drowning. That's not a stable equilibrium for retail spending heading into summer.

items.title: Nasdaq best month since dot-com bottom; DRAM ETF broke IBIT record; breadth-price divergence at all-time record
items.idea: Multiple historic positioning signals: April was the best Nasdaq month since October 2002 (dot-com bottom). S&P 6-week rally only matched by Fed launching QE1 after GFC. Roundhill DRAM hit $10B AUM in 30 trading days (beat IBIT record). S&P breadth and price moving in opposite directions 79 trading days this year (record since 1990). 'Most new lows within S&P500 on a day the index poked above prior all-time closing high. Ever. Like EVER, ever.'
items.angle: These are late-cycle technical signals that individually can be rationalized but together form a pattern. Breadth-price divergence at 79 days means the index is doing one thing while the average stock does another — a classic topping structure. Either the largest tech earnings cycle in history is beginning, or this is the last mile before the drop.
items.tweetA: April was the best month for the Nasdaq since October 2002 — the bottom of the dot-com bubble. The only time the S&P gained more in 6 weeks was when the Fed launched QE1 after 2008. Most new lows on a day the index poked above its all-time high: 'EVER, ever.' None of these are normal data points. They're all pointing at the same thing.
items.tweetB: S&P 500 breadth and price are moving in OPPOSITE directions on 79 trading days this year — would easily be a record since 1990. A rising index with falling breadth works for a while. Then suddenly it doesn't. The question is just when the top 10 names' gravitational pull stops absorbing all the selling pressure from the other 490.

items.title: Anthropic raising $30B+ at $900B+ valuation — doubled from $350B in February, IPO potentially October
items.idea: Anthropic in early talks to raise $30B+ at valuation above $900B, more than doubled from $350B in February 2026 (when Google committed $10B and Amazon $5B). Round could close by end of May, surpassing OpenAI's $852B March mark. IPO reportedly on the table as soon as October. Stated need: must make deals to pay for enough computing infrastructure to meet growing demand.
items.angle: At $900B, the only buyers large enough are sovereign wealth funds and biggest institutional allocators. This isn't equity financing — it's a compute procurement mechanism disguised as a fundraise. Round size is set by the NVIDIA order book, not a DCF.
items.tweetA: Anthropic raising at $900B+. OpenAI was at $852B in March. Both companies are raising not because they want equity partners but because they literally cannot buy enough compute without it. This is the most unusual capital formation in history: the round size is set by the NVIDIA order book, not a DCF. Think about what that means for the next 24 months.
items.tweetB: Anthropic: $350B in February → $900B+ in May. That's not a valuation — it's a compute procurement mechanism. The investors aren't buying equity in a company; they're buying access to the infrastructure buildout. And the price keeps going up because the demand keeps going up. Someone is going to be very right or very wrong about this by 2028.

items.title: SpaceX IPO imminent: filing Wednesday, listing June 12, $75B raise at $2T+ — would be largest IPO in history
items.idea: SpaceX approved 5:1 stock split ahead of IPO. Filing publicly as soon as Wednesday, roadshow June 4, pricing June 11, listing June 12. Seeking to raise $75B at valuation above $2 trillion — would be the largest IPO in history (Saudi Aramco raised $29.4B in 2019). At $2T+, SpaceX would instantly rank among the largest companies on earth.
items.angle: Timing is maximally strategic — Musk monetizing into a market at highs during AI frenzy. Starlink $10B+ ARR + launch dominance + Starship + defense contracts make the business genuinely multi-dimensional. But $2T with 30-year yields at 5.15% is the only question that matters.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO timeline: filing Wednesday, roadshow June 4, listing June 12. $75B raise at $2T+ — would be the largest IPO in history by 2.5x. Saudi Aramco raised $29B in 2019. SpaceX wants to raise $75B. The window is now. Whether the demand exists at $2T with 30-year Treasuries at 5.15% is the only question that matters.
items.tweetB: $2 trillion SpaceX. If it prices there, it's instantly one of the most valuable companies on earth — above Berkshire, above TSMC. And it's a rocket company. The AI frenzy made this possible: every incremental dollar chasing secular growth narratives needed a megacap story with actual revenue. Starlink gives it one. The timing is not an accident.

items.title: Crypto deregulation wave: Morgan Stanley via E*Trade 50bps, Schwab spot, Clarity Act, Western Union stablecoin, SEC crypto stocks
items.idea: Multiple crypto regulatory milestones simultaneously: Morgan Stanley crypto via E*Trade at 50bps (undercutting Coinbase/Robinhood/Schwab); Schwab officially launched spot crypto; Clarity Act cleared Senate banking committee; Western Union launched USDPT stablecoin on Solana; Circle raised $222M at $3B FDV for Arc blockchain token; SEC readying plan for trading crypto versions of stocks.
items.angle: Crypto received the regulatory framework it demanded for years — and capital is going elsewhere anyway because AI absorbs everything. The irony: the clearest regulatory opening for crypto in a decade arrives when investor attention is maximally distracted. Western Union stablecoin is the most underrated item — $170B+ in annual transfer volume using Solana rails for actual remittances.
items.tweetA: Crypto just got everything it ever wanted in one week: Morgan Stanley crypto via E*Trade at 50bps, Schwab spot, Clarity Act through Senate banking, Western Union stablecoin on Solana, SEC prepping crypto stock trading. The regulatory infrastructure finally arrived. The capital didn't. Every spare dollar is in AI stocks right now.
items.tweetB: The stablecoin moment that's underrated: Western Union — $170B+ in annual transfer volume — just launched USDPT on Solana. That's not a DeFi experiment. That's one of the world's largest payments networks using crypto rails for actual remittances. The 'crypto has no real use case' argument just got a lot harder to make.

items.title: Goldman RAI at 99th percentile since 1991; retail volumes 12% above Jan 2021; call skew inversion near meme-stock era record
items.idea: Goldman Sachs Risk Appetite Indicator at 99th percentile since 1991 (highest since 2021) with 'more limited upside and larger symmetric tails for Momentum.' Citadel Securities retail cash volumes in May pacing 12% above January 2021 (height of meme-stock mania). Call skew inverted in 1/3 of S&P top 100 stocks (1-month tenor) and 1/5 for 3-month tenor, approaching the 2021 meme-stock era record of 25%. Crypto saw $1.07B outflows — first negative week in seven.
items.angle: Call skew inversion is the most technically significant signal: retail demand for upside so extreme that calls trade richer than equivalent puts. Last time this widespread: early 2021. RAI at 99th percentile means statistically this is as bullish as markets get. This is not a bearish signal per se — it's a fragility signal.
items.tweetA: Goldman's Risk Appetite Indicator: 99th percentile since 1991. Call skew inverted in 1/3 of S&P top 100 stocks — approaching the 2021 meme-stock era record. Retail volumes 12% above Jan 2021. You can say the AI thesis is different from 2021. You cannot say the positioning is different. It's nearly identical to the 2021 peak.
items.tweetB: When call skew inverts, retail demand for upside has gotten so extreme that calls trade RICHER than equivalent puts. You're paying a premium for optimism. That's happening in 1/3 of the top 100 names right now. Last time it was this widespread: early 2021. The setup isn't bearish. It's fragile. There's a meaningful difference.

charts.desc: 5 charts including Goldman RAI at 99th pct, retail activity vs Jan 2021, call skew inversion. The RAI chart alone is worth saving.

items.title: Ebola: American positive in DRC (Bundibugyo strain — no deployed vaccine), WHO PHEIC, 336 suspected cases, US evacuating to Germany
items.idea: A US citizen working in DRC tested positive for Ebola. 6 Americans total exposed. 336 suspected cases, 88 deaths. WHO declared PHEIC for Congo/Uganda. Critical: the Bundibugyo strain has no widely deployed pre-positioned vaccine — unlike rVSV-ZEBOV used for Zaire ebolavirus. US flying infected American and high-risk contacts to German biocontainment. Goma — major logistics hub — is in the outbreak zone. WHA meeting this week is the next coordinated response trigger.
items.angle: When you can't ring-vaccinate fast (no Bundibugyo vaccine), you rely on movement restrictions and healthcare capacity in a conflict zone where both are severely constrained. The US evacuation to Germany means field containment is already conceded as insufficient. The 'under-detected early weeks' problem compounds everything.
items.tweetA: The Ebola update nobody is treating as serious: the US is NOT trying to contain this in eastern DRC. They're flying Americans to German biocontainment. That's evacuation, not containment. 336 suspected cases, 88 deaths, WHO PHEIC, Bundibugyo strain with no deployed vaccine, Goma as a transit hub. This is harder to stop than 2014.
items.tweetB: Critical Ebola detail: this is the Bundibugyo strain — not Zaire ebolavirus. There is no widely deployed ring-vaccination protocol for Bundibugyo. rVSV-ZEBOV doesn't cover it. When you can't vaccinate fast, you impose movement restrictions on a conflict zone where governance has collapsed. WHA meeting this week is the last coordinated trigger.

items.title: Semis crack: SOX -2.5% Mon/-4% Fri; $100B leveraged ETF exposure = $4B forced selling per 2% move; crowd still hadn't hedged
items.idea: SOX printing biggest downside candle since melt-up began, breaking below steep trend line. Semis ~20% of all hedge fund net exposure (GS Prime). Leveraged ETFs hold ~$100B long semi exposure = ~$2B daily short gamma. A 2% move in semis mechanically triggers ~$4B of forced buying or selling. SMH 3-month ATM vol at 100th percentile coming into the wobble, yet crowd was still chasing upside rather than hedging downside. SOXL attracting novice traders.
items.angle: The gamma mechanics are the critical insight: $4B of forced selling triggered by a -2% day doesn't care about earnings beats. It's mechanical flow. The melt-up was partly reflexive; the unwind will also be partly reflexive. With semis at 20% of HF net exposure and the crowd unhedged at 100th percentile vol, structural setup is for asymmetric downside if NVDA disappoints.
items.tweetA: Semis are ~20% of all hedge fund net exposure. Leveraged ETFs hold ~$100B of long semi positions. A 2% DOWN day in $SOX triggers ~$4B of forced mechanical SELLING into the close. The same flows that powered the melt-up work symmetrically in reverse. And that's before any fundamental catalyst. The NVDA bar is very high. The positioning is very stretched.
items.tweetB: SOXL attracted retail traders who think they understand 3x products. They don't. The $100B in leveraged semi ETFs creates $2B of daily short gamma. A 2% move = $4B of forced rebalancing. The melt-up was partly mechanical. The unwind will also be partly mechanical. Mechanical flows don't stop to read the earnings release.

charts.desc: 4 charts: SOX breakdown candle, hedge fund positioning in semis (GS Prime), SMH vol at 100th percentile, SOXL flow. The HF concentration chart is worth saving.

items.title: Gold approaching $4,400 critical inflection; Japan long-end overshoot creates potential squeeze setup
items.idea: Gold in prolonged consolidation after violent moves earlier in 2026. $4,400 is the next major test with 200-day MA just below — 'must hold area.' Gold tracked Japanese rates through 2025 but Japan long end has now exploded higher and overshot gold instead. European equity vol gapping vs VIX since oil explosion — Europe as the geopolitical risk receiver.
items.angle: The Japan yield / gold divergence is the setup worth watching. If Japan's fiscal situation forces structural BoJ adjustment (which 4.41% 40-year yield strongly implies), gold should eventually close the gap. Divergences like this usually close fast.
items.tweetA: Gold has been tracking Japanese rates tightly for 18 months. Then Japan's 40-year yield exploded to 4.41% — an all-time high — and gold didn't follow. That divergence usually closes. Either Japan rates come back down (unlikely given the fiscal math) or gold catches up. $4,400 is the must-hold level. The 200-day MA is right there.
items.tweetB: Nobody's connecting these two: Japan 40-year at all-time highs signals fiscal stress. Gold is the classic fiscal stress hedge. Gold has been consolidating while JGBs price crisis. When that divergence closes, it closes fast. The $4,400 area is the technical line — break above and gold goes looking for new highs.

charts.desc: Gold at $4,400 vs 200-day MA, and Gold vs Japan 10-year divergence chart. Japan/Gold divergence is worth saving.

items.title: 30-year UST at 5.15% (post-2007 high), S&P at 27.1x vs equal-weight 18.4x; Trump paused Iran attack; SOX worst two sessions since October
items.idea: Monday recap: 30-year UST closed at 5.15% (post-2007 high), 10-year at 4.62% (highest since Feb 2025). SOX -2.5% Mon after -4% Fri = worst two consecutive sessions since October. Trump posted on Truth Social a planned Tuesday attack on Iran was 'scheduled for tomorrow' but paused at request of Mid-East leaders. Only the Dow finished green (+0.3%). S&P 500 at 27.1x earnings vs equal-weight at 18.4x (32% discount). NAHB housing index below 50 for 25th consecutive month.
items.angle: The 32% valuation discount of equal-weight vs cap-weight S&P coexists with 5.15% 30-year yields. Either AI earnings deliver enough to justify 27x with 5% long rates, or the cap-weighted index has a lot of mean-reversion ahead. The Iran 'pause' that couldn't hold a rally is the tell — 'good news' bid is exhausting.
items.tweetA: US 30-year yield: 5.15% (post-2007 high). S&P 500 trading at 27.1x earnings. Equal-weight S&P at 18.4x — a 32% discount. These three things cannot simultaneously be correct about the future. Either AI delivers enough earnings to justify 27x with 5% long rates, or the concentration unwind is coming. Bond market and equity market are telling different stories.
items.tweetB: Trump paused the Iran attack and the Nasdaq still closed -0.5%. The 'good news' bid is failing. When geopolitical de-escalation can't hold a rally, it means bond market pressure is becoming impossible for equities to ignore. 10-year at 4.62%, 30-year at 5.15% — that's competition for capital that AI optimism can't outrun indefinitely.

items.title: Ethereum Foundation faces wave of high-profile departures; BTC to $76K, ETH below $2,100 over weekend
items.idea: Wave of high-profile departures from the Ethereum Foundation prompting community scrutiny. BTC traded as low as $76K, ETH below $2,100 over the weekend. Vitalik Buterin advocating for AI-powered formal verification to make crypto safer. Galaxy Digital received BitLicense from New York State. Ethereum History project building Wikipedia-like ecosystem archive.
items.angle: EF departures pattern matters more than the headline. Ethereum's governance has always relied on the EF as the quiet coordination mechanism. When institutional knowledge exits in waves during a period of L2 fragmentation, Solana competition, and stablecoin legislation, the question is who fills the coordination function.
items.tweetA: The Ethereum Foundation is bleeding researchers and contributors. The community is asking why. The harder question: Ethereum's governance has always relied on the EF as the quiet coordinator. When the EF's human capital rotates out in waves, who fills that coordination function? This matters more for ETH long-term than the price action at $2,100.
items.tweetB: BTC to $76K over the weekend, ETH below $2,100. And the EF is losing contributors simultaneously. These two things are not unrelated — when core infrastructure looks uncertain and capital is flowing elsewhere (AI stocks), the bid weakens from both sides. Vitalik's answer: AI-powered formal verification. Right direction technically, wrong timeframe for the market.

items.title: NVIDIA 13F Q1 2026: CoreWeave +47M shares ($99.4B backlog, 1GW power), new positions in Coherent (optical) and Generate Biomedicines
items.idea: NVIDIA's Q1 2026 13F: largest holding is Intel (manufacturing/packaging/domestic capacity). CoreWeave position increased ~47M shares — Q1 revenue $2.078B (more than doubled YoY), $99.4B backlog, managing over 1 GW of power. New position: Coherent (optical communications/photonics) — addressing the 'bandwidth wall' in large AI training clusters. New position: Generate Biomedicines (AI into biopharma). Three phases: Phase 1 = GPU shortage; Phase 2 = efficiency/networking/power; Phase 3 = high-value applications.
items.angle: The Coherent (optical) position is the most forward-looking signal. As cluster sizes grow, the binding constraint shifts from compute to interconnect — how fast you can move data between thousands of GPUs. NVIDIA is publishing their Phase 2 roadmap in a public regulatory filing. Anyone playing AI on raw GPU count is playing Phase 1.
items.tweetA: NVIDIA's 13F just published their roadmap: largest holding is Intel (domestic packaging), +47M shares CoreWeave ($99.4B backlog, 1 GW power), new position in Coherent (optical interconnects). The GPU shortage was Phase 1. Phase 2 is the bandwidth wall — how fast can you move data between GPUs at massive scale? NVIDIA is telling you exactly which bet to make next.
items.tweetB: CoreWeave Q1 revenue: $2.078B, more than doubled YoY, $99.4B backlog, managing 1 GW of power. NVIDIA just added 47M shares. But the Coherent (optical) new position is what I find more interesting — it says the next bottleneck after power is data throughput inside the cluster. $COHR is Phase 2 AI infrastructure. Most investors are still playing Phase 1.

items.title: NextEra to buy Dominion Energy in $400B+ deal — potentially the largest utility merger in history
items.idea: NextEra Energy announced a deal to acquire Dominion Energy that could create a combined entity worth over $400 billion — potentially the largest utility sector deal ever. Both companies have been major beneficiaries of AI data center power demand. Announced Monday alongside 10-year Treasury hitting 52-week high.
items.angle: This consolidation signals major utilities view AI power demand as structural, multi-decade, and requiring scale to serve. The combined $400B entity would have enormous leverage over where AI data centers physically locate. Energy utilities are now AI infrastructure companies with regulated returns.
items.tweetA: NextEra is buying Dominion in what could be a $400B+ deal — the biggest utility merger in history. Both have been riding the AI data center power demand wave. The consolidation tells you what they believe: the demand is structural, not cyclical, and scale is required to meet it. Energy utilities are now AI infrastructure.
items.tweetB: The $400B NextEra-Dominion deal is the clearest signal yet that energy = AI infrastructure. You can't run a data center without power. The utility companies figured this out before most investors did. And now they're consolidating to ensure they can meet demand at the scale the 2030s AI build-out will require.

items.title: SMH functioning like a commodity: vol rising WITH price — and PURR sees Goldman Sachs + 1M share institutional buyer
items.idea: Capital Flows identifies structural anomaly: SMH implied vol is rising alongside price, not inversely as in normal equity markets. In commodity markets, vol rises with price when buyers are forced to participate regardless of cost. Companies and capital cannot afford to not be long the AI build-out. PURR (Hyperliquid token): Goldman Sachs initiated a position and a single institutional buyer took 1 million shares last week — described as the cleanest early institutional confirmation signal with no VC overhang.
items.angle: The 'commodity flow pattern on equities' is a regime-change marker. When capital is compelled to own an asset regardless of price (think crude in a supply emergency), vol rises with price rather than falling. Normal hedging breaks down. The PURR development is significant: no VC pre-allocation means no legacy sellers, and Goldman initiating is the institutional bridge.
items.tweetA: Here's the tell: $SMH implied vol is rising ALONGSIDE price. In equity markets, vol rises when price falls. In commodity markets, vol rises when price rises because buyers are forced to pay any price. When your semiconductor ETF starts trading like crude in a supply emergency, you're not in a normal equity market anymore. Different rules apply.
items.tweetB: PURR (Hyperliquid): Goldman Sachs initiated a position. A single institutional buyer took 1M shares last week. No VC overhang because there was no pre-sale — nobody has a lower cost basis than you. When Wall Street's largest banks start touching a crypto token with no allocated pre-sale, the 'it's just retail speculation' narrative is officially over.

items.title: China economic warfare playbook: yuan suppression, rare earth controls, dollar substitution, AI supply chain — one coordinated strategy
items.idea: Capital Flows previews deep dive into China's economic warfare strategy: yuan suppression (effective exports subsidy), rare earth export controls (tech choke point), Belt and Road as dollar substitution engine, AI supply chain leverage. Framing: these are not separate policies but parts of one coordinated playbook — the same way US tariff regime is one side of a multi-dimensional economic competition.
items.angle: Rare earth angle is most actionable: China controls 60%+ of rare earth processing and has demonstrated willingness to use that leverage as a geopolitical weapon. When AI supply chain + rare earths + yuan management are viewed as weapons in a coordinated system, every US-China diplomatic headline needs to be read differently.
items.tweetA: Tomorrow's framework worth watching: China's economic warfare playbook — yuan suppression, rare earth export controls, dollar substitution, AI supply chain leverage. These aren't separate policies. They're a coordinated strategy. Understanding them as a system changes how you read every US-China headline. And there are a lot of those right now.
items.tweetB: Everyone talks about China trade war as tariffs. The deeper game: yuan suppression (exports subsidy), rare earth controls (tech choke point), BRI as dollar substitution engine, AI supply chain leverage as negotiating chip. These are weapons, not policies. Investors who model them separately are fighting the last war.

items.title: Cybersecurity moat divergence: PANW and CRWD ripping to new highs; IGV vol elevated = massive vol crush when Intuit prints
items.idea: Capital Flows' February report predicted AI disruption thesis would separate software moat-holders from the rest. Thesis now playing out: PANW and CRWD — both with proprietary threat intelligence data moats — have ripped to new highs while rest of IGV stays suppressed. IGV implied vol at January levels without resolving. Intuit print this week is the catalyst: if Intuit's proprietary tax data provides an AI moat, vol crushes bullishly. If not, suppressed names stay suppressed.
items.angle: PANW and CRWD win because their threat intelligence data is proprietary, continuously updated, and cannot be replicated with a foundation model alone. Names without equivalent proprietary data assets are staying flat. This is a multi-year structural trade, not a momentum position.
items.tweetA: Palo Alto and CrowdStrike are ripping to new highs while the rest of software stays flat. This is the AI moat thesis playing out exactly as predicted: companies with proprietary data (threat intel, customer history) are getting bid. Those without aren't. The bifurcation is not a 2025 story — it's a multi-year structural separation. Watch Intuit this week for the next test.
items.tweetB: IGV implied vol has been at January levels for weeks without resolving. That sets up a massive vol crush the moment Intuit gives clarity on whether AI disrupts or enhances its tax/accounting business. The vol surface says: the market has zero conviction on which half of software wins. The Intuit print this week is the first real data point.

items.title: Callum Thomas: bullish software + BTC, bearish USD; Global/Small/Value rotation stalled but thesis intact
items.idea: Top Down Charts weekly institutional preview: (1) Software: bullish on technicals and major valuation reset. (2) Bitcoin: bullish on seasonal tailwinds, bullish technicals, supportive leading indicators, and 'big reset in price and positioning.' (3) USD: bearish bias given technicals, overvaluation, fading yield support — but needs actual breakdown to raise conviction. (4) Global ex-US: rotation from US/large/growth to global/small/value still favored on relative value — but progress remains 'stop-start.'
items.angle: Callum's Bitcoin bullishness notable given broader market crypto apathy. He cites seasonal tailwinds and positioning reset while crypto is crowded out by AI stocks. BTC bullish + USD bearish is effectively the same trade — if the dollar weakens from current levels, BTC at $77K starts to look like the setup you want.
items.tweetA: Callum Thomas / Topdown Charts: bullish Bitcoin (seasonal tailwinds, positioning reset, technicals) and bearish USD (overvaluation, fading yield support). These two calls are the same thesis. If the dollar weakens from here, BTC at $77K starts to look like a setup, not a problem. The timing on both is unclear — but the direction aligns.
items.tweetB: The Global/Small/Value vs US/Large/Growth rotation: the relative value case is overwhelming. But it's been overwhelming for three years while the trade hasn't worked. At some point mean-reversion happens. Software valuations resetting. USD flat-to-weak. Global earnings improving. The setup is there. The catalyst still isn't. But these things move fast when they move.
